edit.brief: Pointer to the current editing session associated with the character.
edit.details: This variable points to an Edit object that encapsulates the state and behavior of an active editing session for the character. It is initialized to nullptr when no editing session is in progress. The variable is checked to determine whether the character is currently engaged in editing activities, and it is assigned a new Edit instance when a new editing session begins, such as editing descriptions, notes, or help data. By managing this pointer, the system can control, access, and update the editing state and related information throughout various editing operations.
edit.notes: The variable is typically null when idle and points to a dynamically allocated Edit object during active sessions, ensuring proper session management and memory handling.
edit.rationale: This variable exists to track and manage the character's current editing context, enabling consistent control over editing workflows and preventing conflicts or overlapping sessions.

Character::Character().brief: Initializes a new Character instance with default attributes, states, and settings.
Character::Character().details: The Character constructor sets up a new character object by assigning default values to its various attributes and states. It records the current system time as the logon timestamp and sets the pagination line count to a predefined constant PAGELEN. The constructor initializes the armor base array for four armor categories to 100, indicating full armor protection. The character's position is set to POS_STANDING, representing an active, upright stance. Core attributes such as hit points (hit), mana, and stamina (stam) are initialized to their default values of 20, 100, and 100 respectively, using the ATTR_BASE macro combined with the stat_to_attr function to correctly map stats to attribute identifiers. Furthermore, all other core statistics are set to a baseline value of 13, providing a uniform starting point for new characters and ensuring consistency across the game system.
Character::Character().notes: The constructor uses the ATTR_BASE macro and stat_to_attr function to systematically initialize core attributes, ensuring consistency and maintainability. The armor_base array is explicitly set for four categories, aligning with game mechanics that consider multiple armor types. The position is set to POS_STANDING, establishing a default active state for new characters.
Character::Character().rationale: This constructor is used during character creation to establish a standardized starting state, ensuring that all new characters begin with consistent attributes and statuses suitable for immediate gameplay.
Character::Character().name: Character

Character::~Character().brief: Destructor for the Character class that performs comprehensive cleanup of all associated resources, objects, and relationships.
Character::~Character().details: This destructor method ensures proper deallocation of all resources linked to a Character object. It begins by removing and freeing every object the character is carrying, as well as those stored in the locker and strongbox if the character is a player. It clears the character's list of active affects to prevent memory leaks. The function then stops any ongoing tailing relationships, both active and passive, by deleting associated Tail objects and updating relevant flags, ensuring no dangling pointers remain. It also deletes the current editing session if present, and releases dynamically allocated memory for pcdata, apply_cache, defense_mod, and affect_cache, calling specialized functions to handle cache cleanup. Throughout, it carefully manages linked list traversal and object removal to maintain game state integrity and prevent resource leaks, handling both NPC and player characters appropriately.

params.pcdata: Pointer to the player's data structure, used to access locker and strongbox objects for cleanup.
params.obj_next: Temporary pointer to hold the next Object in the list during iteration.
params.obj: Pointer used to iterate through and extract each Object carried or stored in locker and strongbox.
params.edit: Pointer to the current editing session data associated with the character, deleted if not null.
params.td: Pointer to individual Tail objects during tail cleanup.
params.tail: Pointer to the linked list of tailing relationships, which are stopped and deleted.

Character::~Character().returns: This function has no return value; it performs cleanup and deallocates resources associated with the Character object.
Character::~Character().notes: The destructor carefully handles both NPC and player characters, ensuring all dynamically allocated memory and linked relationships are properly released. Special attention is given to stopping tailing activities and clearing affect lists to maintain consistency. The cleanup process prevents memory leaks and dangling pointers, supporting stable game state management.
Character::~Character().rationale: This destructor is essential for safely removing a character from the game, ensuring all associated resources are properly freed, especially during character deletion or logout, to maintain system stability and prevent memory leaks.
Character::~Character().name: ~Character

void Character::update().brief: Updates the state and behaviors of a character, primarily NPCs, including triggers, movement, and resource management.
void Character::update().details: The function performs a comprehensive update cycle for a character, focusing on NPCs that are in valid rooms and not affected by charm effects. It first checks if the character's position is at least sitting; if not, it halts further processing. For active NPCs, it assesses whether the room's area contains any players or immortals; if none are present, it skips actions to optimize performance. The function then executes any assigned special behavior functions and triggers mob programs, including random and area-wide triggers, which can modify the character's state or initiate actions. If the character has an associated shop and lacks sufficient funds, it replenishes their gold and silver based on their prototype data, simulating shopkeeper resource management. For NPCs with the scavenger act flag, it attempts to loot the most valuable takeable and lootable object in the room, with a probabilistic chance. Additionally, if the NPC is not flagged as a sentinel, it may randomly move to an adjacent room, considering environmental constraints such as door states, indoor/outdoor flags, and area boundaries. The update ensures actions are contextually appropriate, enabling autonomous and dynamic behaviors for NPCs within the game environment.

params.this: Pointer to the Character object being updated.

void Character::update().returns: Void; the function performs side effects such as state updates, movement, and actions without returning a value.
void Character::update().notes: The function incorporates probabilistic checks for scavenging and movement, and respects environmental flags and character states to ensure realistic NPC behaviors. It also handles resource replenishment for shopkeeper NPCs, maintaining economic consistency.
void Character::update().rationale: This function is used to simulate autonomous NPC behaviors, including movement, scavenging, and resource management, enabling a lively and responsive game world as demonstrated in various NPC scripts and behaviors across the codebase.
void Character::update().name: update

bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.brief: Checks if a player character has any of the specified cgroup flags set.
bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.details: This function determines whether a character belongs to any of the specified cgroup groups by examining their cgroup flags. It first verifies if the character is an NPC; if so, it returns false since NPCs do not have cgroup flags. For player characters, it accesses the 'pcdata' member to retrieve the cgroup flags and utilizes the 'has_any_of' method to check for any overlap with the provided flags. This mechanism enables efficient permission and role verification based on group memberships, which are essential for managing access rights, roles, and statuses within the game environment.

params.cg: A Flags object representing the set of cgroup flags to check for.

bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.returns: True if the character is a player and has at least one of the specified cgroup flags; false otherwise.
bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.notes: The function explicitly excludes NPCs from cgroup checks, ensuring that only player characters are evaluated for group memberships. It relies on the 'pcdata' pointer being valid for player characters, which is a common design pattern for differentiating player and non-player entities.
bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.rationale: This function is used in scenarios where the game needs to verify a character's group-based permissions or roles, such as in command authorization, role assignments, or status displays. Its efficient check helps maintain clear access controls and role management within the game's logic.
bool Character::has_cgroup(const Flags &.name: has_cgroup

void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.brief: Adds specified group flags to a player character's group flags if the character is not an NPC.
void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.details: The function begins by checking whether the character is a player character through the \u0027is_npc()\u0027 method. If the character is indeed a player (i.e., \u0027is_npc()\u0027 returns false), it adds the provided flags \u0027cg\u0027 to the character's \u0027cgroup_flags\u0027 stored within \u0020pcdata. This addition is performed using the \u0027+=\u0027 operator, which is assumed to be overloaded in the \u0027Flags\u0027 class to combine flag sets efficiently. This operation updates the player's group membership or permissions based on the flags provided, while ensuring NPCs' data remains unaffected. The method effectively manages dynamic group affiliations for player characters, supporting permission and role management within the game logic.

params.cg: A constant reference to a Flags object representing the set of group flags to be added.

void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.returns: This function does not return a value.
void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.notes: The function only modifies group flags for player characters, leaving NPCs' data unchanged. It relies on the \u0027+=\u0027 operator being properly overloaded in the Flags class to handle flag combination. This method is typically used during group management, permission escalation, or character initialization processes.
void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.rationale: This function is used whenever a character's group membership or permissions need to be updated dynamically, such as during promotions, role assignments, or loading character data with specific group flags. It ensures that only player characters' group flags are modified, maintaining data integrity for NPCs.
void Character::add_cgroup(const Flags &.name: add_cgroup

void Character::remove_cgroup(const Flag.brief: Removes specified group flags from a player character's group flags if the character is a player.
void Character::remove_cgroup(const Flag.details: This function first checks whether the character is a player-controlled character by verifying that the 'pcdata' pointer is not null, which indicates a player character. If the character is indeed a player, it proceeds to remove the specified flags from the 'cgroup_flags' by subtracting the 'cg' flags, effectively clearing those bits. This operation ensures that only player characters' group flags are modified, leaving NPCs unaffected. The removal process relies on bitwise subtraction, which clears the bits corresponding to the provided flags, maintaining the integrity of other flags. This method allows dynamic management of group memberships and permissions for players within the game environment.

params.cg: The set of flags to be removed from the character's group flags.

void Character::remove_cgroup(const Flag.returns: This function does not return a value.
void Character::remove_cgroup(const Flag.notes: The function explicitly avoids modifying NPCs by checking the 'pcdata' pointer, ensuring that only player characters' group flags are affected. The use of subtraction for flag removal assumes that the flags are managed as bit masks, providing an efficient way to clear specific bits.
void Character::remove_cgroup(const Flag.rationale: This function is used in scenarios such as demoting a player, removing leadership roles, or adjusting group permissions, as evidenced by its usage in commands that modify group affiliations and roles within the game.

bool Character::is_npc() const.brief: Determines whether the character is a non-player character (NPC).
bool Character::is_npc() const.details: This function checks the 'pcdata' pointer to identify if the character is an NPC. If 'pcdata' is null, it indicates that the character does not have player-specific data, classifying it as an NPC. If 'pcdata' is non-null, the character is controlled by a player. This straightforward check allows game logic to differentiate between NPCs and player characters efficiently, influencing behavior, interactions, and game mechanics accordingly.
bool Character::is_npc() const.returns: Returns true if the character is an NPC ('pcdata' is null); otherwise, false.
bool Character::is_npc() const.notes: The function relies on the convention that player characters always have associated 'pcdata', whereas NPCs do not. This method is used extensively in game logic to tailor behavior based on character type.
bool Character::is_npc() const.rationale: Used in various contexts such as combat triggers, behavior checks, and game state management to determine if a character is controlled by a player or is an NPC, thereby influencing subsequent logic and actions.
bool Character::is_npc() const.name: is_npc

virtual const std::string Character::ide.brief: Returns the character's name used as its primary identifier.
virtual const std::string Character::ide.details: This function accesses the 'name' member variable of the Character class and returns it as a constant string reference. Being declared as virtual, it allows derived classes to override this method to provide alternative identification mechanisms if needed. The returned name serves as the main identifier for the character within the game environment, facilitating messaging, command processing, and interaction logic that rely on character identification.
virtual const std::string Character::ide.returns: The character's name as a constant string.
virtual const std::string Character::ide.notes: Since the function returns a reference to the internal 'name' string, it avoids copying and ensures efficient access. Overriding this method allows customization of character identification in subclasses.
virtual const std::string Character::ide.rationale: This function is used whenever the system needs to retrieve a character's identifier, such as for messaging, command recognition, or interaction handling, ensuring consistent and flexible identification across different character types.
virtual const std::string Character::ide.name: identifier

Character::Character(const Character &).brief: Copy constructor for the Character class.
Character::Character(const Character &).details: This constructor creates a new instance of the Character class by copying the state of an existing Character object. It ensures that the new object is a duplicate of the original, with all member variables initialized to the same values as those in the source object.
Character::Character(const Character &).returns: This constructor does not return a value.
Character::Character(const Character &).name: Character

Character & Character::operator=(const C.brief: Assigns the values from another Character object to this one.
Character & Character::operator=(const C.details: This assignment operator copies the state of the given Character object into the current object. It ensures that all relevant data members are duplicated, effectively making the current object a copy of the provided Character. This operator handles self-assignment and returns a reference to the current object to allow for chaining of assignment operations.

params.rhs: The Character object to be copied from.

Character & Character::operator=(const C.returns: A reference to the current Character object after assignment.
Character & Character::operator=(const C.name: operator=
